These constraints imply that thick epoxy coatings exceed the limits of cross-cut evaluation, necessitating pull-off methods for accurate assessment [CITE:ev_bb2ddf4927283db1].\n\n| Test Method Constraint | Thickness Limit | Unit | Evidence Source |\n| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |\n| Cross-Cut Test Maximum | 250 | micrometers | [CITE:ev_a256fbf9c6062207] |\n| Cross-Cut Test Applicable | 250 | \u00b5m | [CITE:ev_df003953c7f7a2d0] |\n| General Test Method Range | 0.1 to 30 | micrometers | [CITE:ev_8858c4a9dba538b2] |\n| Cross Hatch Laboratory Use | Under 5 | mils | [CITE:ev_c8b75c8d91e7faea] |\n\nTable 1 illustrates the overlapping yet distinct thickness ranges for adhesion evaluation. The variation in units (micrometers vs. mils) across standards complicates direct regulatory compliance comparisons without conversion, though the evidence preserves original units [CITE:ev_c8b75c8d91e7faea]. Tape test results are dependent on paint thickness, affecting comparability between different coating systems [CITE:ev_6666c9bcf54e3c33]. This dependency is particularly relevant when comparing silicone materials, which often utilize thinner conformal layers, against structural epoxy coatings [CITE:ev_1377196d9ff7c638]. Consequently, thickness control is not merely a procedural step but a determinant of method validity [CITE:ev_3550bf7b3e0ae5a8].\n\nCuring conditions prior to testing significantly influence adhesion performance metrics. Roof coating must cure a minimum of 72 hours before conducting a peel adhesion test [CITE:ev_9cc92ec315452ccc]. Premature testing risks measuring cohesive strength of the uncured polymer rather than adhesive bond strength to the substrate [CITE:ev_2b7ca35715831266]. Pull-off adhesion testing measures adhesive strength of coatings after application and curing per guidelines [CITE:ev_2b7ca35715831266]. The quality of coating application varies by coater and impacts adhesion results [CITE:ev_315057e2cc7dd3f8]. Standard testing procedures evaluate peel force under constant conditions to minimize environmental variance [CITE:ev_be43a72512267deb]. These environmental controls are essential when comparing temperature-sensitive silicone materials against more rigid epoxy systems [CITE:ev_1377196d9ff7c638].\n\nConflicting evidence regarding delamination rates highlights the sensitivity of preparation variables. One study reported that PUA coating without silane treatment had 6.75% delamination in a tape peel test [CITE:ev_df0f1276d85b68da]. In direct contradiction, another source states that PUA+4SR coating without silane treatment showed approximately 95% delamination (94.93%) [CITE:ev_379a35c45a4fa40f]. This vast divergence suggests that minor variations in nanoparticle filling or substrate preparation drastically alter failure modes [CITE:ev_379a35c45a4fa40f]. The position supporting higher delamination rates aligns with evidence that low adhesion strength of silicones was apparent in results showing little dependence on peel speed [CITE:ev_1377196d9ff7c638]. Therefore, the 95% delamination figure likely reflects the inherent difficulty in bonding silicone-modified systems without coupling agents, whereas the 6.75% figure represents a specific formulation variant [CITE:ev_df0f1276d85b68da].\n\nFurther conflict exists regarding the fundamental capability of testing methods to measure adhesion. One source states the pull-off adhesion test measures the strength of the bond between a coating and the surface [CITE:ev_d62d99d84a009e00]. However, contradictory evidence asserts that the test method does not measure fundamental adhesion strength of the bond between coating and substrate [CITE:ev_1a095db8b38db71d]. This disagreement stems from the distinction between practical adhesion and thermodynamic adhesion [CITE:ev_1a095db8b38db71d]. Pull-off testing measures coating resistance to separation using perpendicular tensile force, which includes cohesive failure components [CITE:ev_f99cba7616963c64]. Cross-cut testing measures coating resistance to separation by cutting a right-angle lattice pattern to the substrate [CITE:ev_0c29c65200b8e0c0]. The Cross-Cut Test provides qualitative evaluation while the Pull-Off Method provides quantitative evaluation of coating adhesion [CITE:ev_84a08742318ed15d]. Thus, while pull-off tests yield numerical data, they do not necessarily isolate the interfacial bond strength from the bulk material strength [CITE:ev_d62d99d84a009e00].\n\nPreparation for cross-cut tests involves specific geometric constraints to ensure valid classification. The cross-cut test uses a tool to cut a right-angle lattice pattern through the coating to the substrate [CITE:ev_183b421be46e5e8b]. This lattice pattern cut into the coating down to the substrate facilitates adhesive tape removal [CITE:ev_3fadc73633d06ff8]. The Cross-Cut Test classifies adhesion results into six levels according to the degree of peeling after tape removal [CITE:ev_cd6ffc422440dd18]. Results are assessed on a 0A-5A scale where 5A indicates no peeling and 0A indicates complete removal [CITE:ev_5311f47cd26b20c1]. Cross-cut adhesion test evaluation ranges from 0B representing complete detachment to 5B representing no detachment [CITE:ev_d0d69c4b450fa1e3]. The variation in scaling (0A-5A vs 0B-5B) across evidence sources indicates potential inconsistencies in standard adoption between ASTM and ISO frameworks [CITE:ev_815bc543dbac8f04]. ASTM D3359 outlines standardized test methods for evaluating adhesion of coatings to metallic substrates using pressure-sensitive tape [CITE:ev_815bc543dbac8f04].\n\nValid pull-off test execution requires specific coverage criteria to ensure data integrity. A valid pull-off test requires coating to cover at least 50% of stamp surface after detachment [CITE:ev_d0994f38fa60bebe]. If the failure occurs within the adhesive used to attach the dolly rather than the coating itself, the data is invalid [CITE:ev_d0994f38fa60bebe]. The study objective was to identify an optimal adhesive for the pull-off test providing higher adhesion [CITE:ev_f8a2b17c15a40670]. This underscores that the testing adhesive itself is a variable in sample preparation [CITE:ev_f8a2b17c15a40670]. Pull-off force thickness dependence agreed with modified Kendall model for elastomeric coatings, linking physical preparation to theoretical modeling [CITE:ev_19047670aed8064b].\n\nSurface preparation extends beyond coating application to include substrate conditioning. Site adhesion testing must be verified according to criteria to confirm proper surface preparation [CITE:ev_97840fd9469c02ca]. Paint and tape adhesion tests are useful for screening surface prep and cure on coatings [CITE:ev_be43a72512267deb]. Surface free energy and adhesion parameter measurements are proposed as complementary methods to traditional cross-cut tape tests [CITE:ev_d77bb9a950ab7132]. This suggests that physical cutting methods may not fully capture the chemical readiness of the substrate, particularly for low-energy surfaces like silicone [CITE:ev_d77bb9a950ab7132]. Adhesion test must be conducted prior to estimating coating restoration project per Inland adhesion testing procedures [CITE:ev_61c41c390dad6a70].\n\nThe implications of these preparation variables are critical for the comparative analysis of epoxy, urethane, and silicone. Epoxy coatings, often thicker, align well with pull-off protocols but exceed cross-cut thickness limits [CITE:ev_a256fbf9c6062207]. Silicone materials, frequently applied as thin conformal coatings, fit within cross-cut parameters but exhibit low adhesion strength in peel tests [CITE:ev_1377196d9ff7c638]. Peel and adhesion strength testing measures bond strength when force attempts separation [CITE:ev_27f9ac91a5f22e9b]. Peel testing is suitable for designs where bonded edges experience prying or bending [CITE:ev_1be768df70c46a07].\n\nMeasurement units and reporting standards further complicate cross-method comparison. Peel strength is calculated using force applied during peeling process divided by film width [CITE:ev_1ae4530e37be2c91]. Scrape test adhesion is measured in kilograms of force required for removal [CITE:ev_73f6c6e661c92e30]. Pull-off method provides values for peel stress allowing quick determination and comparison of coating adhesion [CITE:ev_03d5cf9642fafd22]. However, peel-back and pull-off data should not be compared since testing mechanisms differ [CITE:ev_aabe5f967b113f45]. This prohibition is vital for the research question; combining pull-off MPa data with peel N/mm data without mechanistic context yields erroneous conclusions about material superiority [CITE:ev_aabe5f967b113f45].\n\nIndependent laboratories have noted differences of up to 50 percent in tape adhesion variability over time [CITE:ev_ce3124033536bdb1]. This variability emphasizes the need for strict thickness and curing controls when benchmarking polymer coatings [CITE:ev_ce3124033536bdb1]. Crosshatch testing is most suitable for thin coatings on rigid substrates [CITE:ev_3550bf7b3e0ae5a8]. The cross-cut test is especially suitable for thin layers on rigid substrates like metal or plastic [CITE:ev_bb2ddf4927283db1]. Four common methods exist for testing adhesion of protective coatings and linings per ASTM standards [CITE:ev_c53e1e15e6c509b0]. Tape and pull-off tests are common methods for evaluating coating adhesion to substrates [CITE:ev_fee4deed784b02c8].\n\nThe selection of preparation method dictates the failure mode observed. Adhesion is rated by inspecting the cut area for coating removal relative to classifications [CITE:ev_d23f5ac9162865a3]. Mode of failure and pull-off pressure confirm proper surface preparation and coating application [CITE:ev_399c2dba04cb99b8]. Test method covers determination of practical adhesion strength of hard ceramic coatings on metal and ceramic substrates [CITE:ev_f6c7a8ecd3f37045]. Using a calibrated knife reduced subjectivity found in other adhesion tests [CITE:ev_5caae816b34f9fb7]. This reduction in subjectivity is crucial for regulatory compliance where binary pass/fail criteria are enforced [CITE:ev_5caae816b34f9fb7].\n\nIn the context of silicone roofing, specific preparation mandates exist. Peel adhesion test kit measures force required to pull coating off primed surface [CITE:ev_092f7ee5d2103ef4]. Hot water and knife adhesion tests were validated as valuable tools for quality control in this study [CITE:ev_cca3f9b996f96bd9]. Hot water and knife tests effectively discriminated between good and bad quality coatings [CITE:ev_6d68babb48b5d010]. Results showed good agreement between hot water-adhesion tests and TxDOT peel tests [CITE:ev_6c07ce38730c09f8]. These alternative preparation methods offer robustness against environmental variables that standard peel tests might not capture [CITE:ev_6c07ce38730c09f8].\n\nUltimately, sample preparation acts as the filter through which material performance is viewed. Inconsistent thickness control invalidates comparisons between epoxy and silicone systems due to their differing mechanical responses to stress [CITE:ev_19047670aed8064b]. The evidence supports a structured approach where thickness limits define the method, and curing times define the validity [CITE:ev_9cc92ec315452ccc]. Adhesive formulations are designed to provide excellent adhesion and high shear and peel strength [CITE:ev_8711bd68ae7dca95]. Comprehensive adhesive suitability requires comparing peel adhesion, tensile strength, and shear resistance [CITE:ev_ac671c6a322fe8bd]. By adhering to strict preparation protocols, the comparative analysis of polymer coatings can isolate material properties from procedural artifacts [CITE:ev_ac671c6a322fe8bd].\n\n**Key Findings**\n\n*   Cross-cut test methods are limited to coating thicknesses up to 250 micrometers, restricting their use for thick epoxy systems compared to pull-off methods [CITE:ev_a256fbf9c6062207].\n*   Conflicting evidence regarding delamination rates (6.75% vs 95% for PUA without silane) indicates high sensitivity to nanoparticle filling and substrate preparation variables [CITE:ev_df0f1276d85b68da].\n*   Peel-back and pull-off data cannot be directly compared due to differing testing mechanisms, necessitating separate evaluation tracks for epoxy, urethane, and silicone materials [CITE:ev_aabe5f967b113f45].\n*   Valid pull-off testing requires at least 50% coating coverage on the stamp surface after detachment to ensure measurement of coating adhesion rather than adhesive failure [CITE:ev_d0994f38fa60bebe].\n*   Standard testing procedures mandate constant environmental conditions to minimize variance in peel force measurements [CITE:ev_be43a72512267deb].\n\n![Measurement Parameters](data:image/png;base64,)\n*Figure 1: Bar chart showing numeric values of measurement parameters like hardness and thickness.*\n\n\n\n![Standard Timeline](data:image/png;base64,)\n*Figure 2: Timeline showing adoption, reapproval, and publication years.*\n\n\n\n| Parameter | Value | Unit |\n| --- | --- | --- |\n| Document Page Count | 29 | pages |\n| Vickers Hardness Threshold | 5 | GPa |\n| Coating Thickness Limit | 30 | \u00b5m |\n| Diamond Indenter Included Angle | 120 | degrees |\n| Diamond Indenter Tip Radius | 200 | \u00b5m |\n| Applicable Coating Thickness Minimum | 0.1 | \u00b5m |\n| Applicable Coating Thickness Maximum | 30 | \u00b5m |\n\n*Table 1: Summary of Technical Measurements*\n\n\n## Surface Contaminants and Adhesive Failure Types\n\nSurface contaminants represent a primary variable affecting adhesion test outcomes, as oils, dust, or moisture form a barrier between adhesive and surface weakening bond [CITE:ev_4ef566dfab383143]. The presence of such contaminants directly impacts the measured adhesion strength, with solvent wiping of contaminated surfaces increasing bond strength from 0.8 MPa to 10.9 MPa for Epoxy D [CITE:ev_a3334666bd32dd8a]. This thirteen-fold improvement demonstrates that contamination control is not merely a preparatory step but a determinant of whether testing methods accurately reflect coating performance. Surface energy mismatch prevents proper wetting when substrate energy is lower than adhesive surface energy, creating conditions where adhesion tests indicate failure despite adequate coating formulation [CITE:ev_ea1b9ed2aa2e3a82]. High surface energy materials such as metals and glass promote strong adhesive bonds, whereas low-energy substrates require additional surface treatment to achieve comparable adhesion metrics [CITE:ev_fbc506f470d4da9e]. Untreated PA showed lowest work of adhesion at 69.7 mN/m with highest interfacial tension leading to weak coating retention, quantifying the impact of surface treatment on adhesion parameters [CITE:ev_96b127d3014b3201].\n\nAdhesion strength measurements vary significantly across material types and surface conditions, establishing a performance envelope for structural applications. The following table compares quantitative adhesion values derived from distinct testing contexts and material systems.\n\n| Material/Context | Adhesion Strength | Condition/Standard |\n| :--- | :--- | :--- |\n| Epoxy D | 0.8 MPa | Contaminated Surface [CITE:ev_a3334666bd32dd8a] |\n| Epoxy D | 10.9 MPa | Solvent Wiped [CITE:ev_a3334666bd32dd8a] |\n| Hydroxyapatite | 3.4 \u00b1 1.0 MPa | ASTM Test Methods [CITE:ev_5bd33c7a02d9cd28] |\n| Industrial Adhesives | 10 MPa to over 60 MPa | Structural Applications [CITE:ev_a85f361a2bdff124] |\n\nTesting methodologies respond differently to contamination levels and substrate conditions. Pull-off adhesion test measures the strength of the bond between a coating and the surface it is applied to, providing quantitative values for peel stress allowing quick determination and comparison of coating adhesion [CITE:ev_d62d99d84a009e00][CITE:ev_03d5cf9642fafd22]. The force measured in MPa or psi reveals the coating's tensile pull-off strength indicating adhesion [CITE:ev_11ae17d7fc3f76c6]. However, conflicting evidence suggests this test method does not measure fundamental adhesion strength of the bond between coating and substrate, indicating limitations in what pull-off values actually represent [CITE:ev_1a095db8b38db71d]. This contradiction requires resolution: while pull-off testing provides numerical strength values in MPa or psi revealing the coating's tensile pull-off strength indicating adhesion, these values reflect practical adhesion rather than intrinsic molecular bonding [CITE:ev_11ae17d7fc3f76c6]. ASTM D4541 and BS EN ISO 4624 describe pull-off adhesion testing standards for protective coatings, establishing international consistency for quantitative assessment [CITE:ev_befc6654b3c8f5ad]. ISO 4624 is referenced as a global standard for pull-off adhesion testing compliance, further validating the method for regulatory contexts [CITE:ev_a9eaff2972823a42].\n\nCross-cut testing offers qualitative evaluation contrasting with pull-off quantitative assessment [CITE:ev_84a08742318ed15d]. ASTM D3359 outlines standardized test methods for evaluating adhesion of coatings to metallic substrates using pressure-sensitive tape, with the standard codified in 1974 [CITE:ev_815bc543dbac8f04][CITE:ev_cee97c994fefc9b1]. The cross-cut test classifies adhesion results into six levels according to the degree of peeling after tape removal, providing categorical rather than continuous data [CITE:ev_cd6ffc422440dd18]. ISO 2409 specifies the cross-cut test method for evaluating adhesion of coatings on sheet metal parts, establishing international consistency for this qualitative approach [CITE:ev_067cc0abd42529d2]. JIS K 5600-5-6: 1999 specifies the Cross-Cut Test method for coating adhesion evaluation under Japanese Industrial Standards, further demonstrating global standardization of this method [CITE:ev_38e4b21684c9f29c]. The tape test is qualitative and cannot precisely assess quantitatively the actual strength of the adhesion, limiting its utility for comparing epoxy, urethane, and silicone materials on a continuous scale [CITE:ev_389d441cc3fe3ba4]. Cross hatch tape testing is intended for laboratory use on coatings under 5 mils thickness, restricting field applicability for thicker polymer systems [CITE:ev_c8b75c8d91e7faea].\n\nPeel tests measure adhesive strength by pulling one material away from another at a specific angle and speed, offering a third methodological approach [CITE:ev_f0f1594bc5d9dd20]. Comprehensive adhesive suitability requires comparing peel adhesion, tensile strength, and shear resistance, suggesting no single test method suffices for complete material characterization [CITE:ev_ac671c6a322fe8bd]. Results showed good agreement between hot water-adhesion tests and TxDOT peel tests, indicating peel methods correlate with environmental exposure performance [CITE:ev_6c07ce38730c09f8]. For silicone materials specifically, cure improves adhesion except for silicone, where failure occurred at the tape interface, suggesting peel testing underestimates silicone coating performance [CITE:ev_0db5383239a26b95]. Traditional adhesion tests like ISO/JIS cross-hatch and ASTM tape tests only evaluate final adhesion strength without explaining adhesion mechanisms, limiting diagnostic capability for failure analysis [CITE:ev_60e99e00ac021116]. Surface free energy and adhesion parameter measurements proposed as complementary method to traditional cross-cut tape tests, suggesting contamination assessment requires both traditional testing and surface science approaches [CITE:ev_d77bb9a950ab7132].\n\nFailure mode classification distinguishes between adhesive and cohesive failure, with critical implications for interpreting test results. Adhesive failure indicates molecular attraction between adhesive and substrate was weaker than internal adhesive strength [CITE:ev_1826db32940b1bde]. Adhesive failure occurs when adhesive peels away cleanly from substrate surface indicating poor surface preparation or contamination [CITE:ev_a1b14fef9fc9b89b]. Conversely, cohesive strength decreases as coating undergoes weathering and pull-off strength monitors breakdown, meaning cohesive failure within the coating layer indicates material degradation rather than bond failure [CITE:ev_8001a26420bf8b10]. Acceptable adhesion shows cohesive tear within sealant or elongation beyond movement capability, establishing that cohesive failure within the adhesive layer represents successful bonding [CITE:ev_52375915486842b0]. Glue failure involves visible separation of the glue from itself, the coating, or dolly, providing visual indicators for failure classification [CITE:ev_b53f0a2c5edbc39f]. Mechanical adhesion occurs when adhesive penetrates microscopic ridges and valleys of substrate anchoring the coating, distinguishing mechanical interlocking from chemical bonding mechanisms [CITE:ev_11cf92f65a363078].\n\nMaterial-specific performance varies significantly across epoxy, urethane, and silicone coatings. Nine common adhesives including cyanoacrylic and epoxy were selected and tested on specimens, providing comparative data across adhesive chemistries [CITE:ev_3f84be5fe88e4623]. Most cyanoacrylic adhesives failed with breakage occurring in the adhesive film during testing, indicating cohesive failure modes dominate for this material class [CITE:ev_90cd35013a72b096]. Two different epoxy adhesives were characterized including high elongation high toughness epoxy and toughened epoxy adhesive, demonstrating formulation variations affect failure modes [CITE:ev_61cc13e36041ee9a]. Adhesive formulations are designed to provide excellent adhesion and high shear and peel strength, with performance varying by polymer chemistry [CITE:ev_8711bd68ae7dca95]. Adhesion variance results from surface treatment or the nature of the substrate, emphasizing that test results reflect both coating and substrate conditions [CITE:ev_eb6a7ba09f91b0a1]. Coating application quality varies by coater and impacts adhesion, introducing human factors into test variability [CITE:ev_315057e2cc7dd3f8].\n\nField testing considerations differ from laboratory protocols. Field adhesion tests must be performed at the shop or jobsite to verify sealant adhesion, establishing location requirements for compliance [CITE:ev_5da5d464178b2eea]. Field adhesion test also called hand pull test verifies proper sealant installation, providing rapid on-site assessment capability [CITE:ev_7658ca702fb6e4fa]. Field adhesion test recommended when laboratory test cannot be performed on-site, acknowledging practical constraints in coating application environments [CITE:ev_f569492a9905b1d9]. Adhesion test must be conducted prior to estimating coating restoration project per Inland adhesion testing procedures, establishing timing requirements for assessment [CITE:ev_61c41c390dad6a70]. Paint and tape adhesion tests are useful for screening surface prep and cure on coatings, indicating these methods serve quality control functions rather than definitive strength measurement [CITE:ev_be43a72512267deb]. Independent laboratories have noted differences of up to 50 percent in tape adhesion variability over time, quantifying inter-laboratory variation that affects result comparability [CITE:ev_ce3124033536bdb1].\n\nAdhesion strength of coating to substrate defines overall coating integrity and performance, establishing the stakes for accurate testing [CITE:ev_2852c4416e3e0b87]. Evaluating adhesion before and after exposure lengths helps estimate adhesion retention, recommending longitudinal testing approaches [CITE:ev_02597b3988622977]. Mode of failure and pull-off pressure confirm proper surface preparation and coating application, linking test outcomes to process verification [CITE:ev_399c2dba04cb99b8]. Failure to verify and document adhesion results may result in adhesion loss not covered by warranty, establishing documentation requirements for liability protection [CITE:ev_a889a20cea5550f0]. All structural applications must be tested by the Dow test lab for surface preparation recommendations, indicating manufacturer-specific protocols for critical applications [CITE:ev_24d4477c6b68a302]. Adhesive bonds can be stronger than the materials being bonded together in some automotive applications, demonstrating that cohesive substrate failure represent optimal bonding performance [CITE:ev_20783c49fa127283]. Valid pull-off test requires coating to cover at least 50% of stamp surface after detachment, establishing validity criteria for quantitative data [CITE:ev_d0994f38fa60bebe]. Test method covers determination of practical adhesion strength of hard ceramic coatings on metal and ceramic substrates, extending applicability beyond polymer systems [CITE:ev_f6c7a8ecd3f37045]. Test method is commonly used for coating thicknesses in the range of 0.1 to 30 micrometers, defining dimensional constraints for accurate assessment [CITE:ev_8858c4a9dba538b2].\n\n**Key Findings**\n\n*   Surface contaminants such as oils, dust, or moisture form barriers between adhesive and surface, with solvent wiping increasing epoxy bond strength from 0.8 MPa to 10.9 MPa, demonstrating contamination control directly determines test outcomes [CITE:ev_4ef566dfab383143][CITE:ev_a3334666bd32dd8a].\n*   Pull-off testing provides quantitative values in MPa or psi for coating adhesion comparison, though evidence conflicts on whether it measures fundamental bond strength versus practical adhesion strength [CITE:ev_11ae17d7fc3f76c6][CITE:ev_d62d99d84a009e00][CITE:ev_1a095db8b38db71d].\n*   Cross-cut testing per ASTM D3359 (codified 1974) and ISO 2409 provides qualitative six-level classification, contrasting with pull-off quantitative assessment and limiting material comparison precision [CITE:ev_cee97c994fefc9b1][CITE:ev_067cc0abd42529d2][CITE:ev_cd6ffc422440dd18].\n*   Adhesive failure indicates weaker molecular attraction between adhesive and substrate than internal adhesive strength, whereas cohesive failure within the adhesive layer represents acceptable adhesion performance [CITE:ev_1826db32940b1bde][CITE:ev_52375915486842b0].\n*   Independent laboratories report up to 50 percent variability in tape adhesion testing, requiring multiple test methods and contamination controls for reliable epoxy, urethane, and silicone material comparison [CITE:ev_ce3124033536bdb1][CITE:ev_ac671c6a322fe8bd].\n\n![Measurement Specifications](data:image/png;base64,)\n*Figure 1: Bar chart showing key measurement values defined in ASTM C1624 standard.*\n\n\n\n![Standard Development Timeline](data:image/png;base64,)\n*Figure 2: Timeline showing adoption, reapproval, and publication years.*\n\n\n\n| Parameter | Value | Unit | Year |\n| --- | --- | --- | --- |\n| Document Page Count | 29 | pages | 2019 |\n| Vickers Hardness Threshold | 5 | GPa | 2010 |\n| Coating Thickness Limit | 30 | \u00b5m | 2010 |\n| Diamond Indenter Included Angle | 120 | degrees | 2010 |\n| Diamond Indenter Tip Radius | 200 | \u00b5m | 2010 |\n| Applicable Coating Thickness Minimum | 0.1 | \u00b5m | 2010 |\n| Applicable Coating Thickness Maximum | 30 | \u00b5m | 2010 |\n\n*Table 1: Summary of Technical Measurements in ASTM C1624*\n\n\n## Adhesion Performance Scoring Scales and Evaluation Criteria\n\nFollowing the analysis of surface contaminants and adhesive failure types, the quantification of these failures requires standardized scoring scales and evaluation criteria to ensure comparability across polymer coating systems. The selection of an appropriate scoring methodology is critical when comparing adhesion testing methods for polymer coatings including pull-off, cross-cut, and peel tests across epoxy, urethane, and silicone materials, as the output metrics dictate the interpretability of coating integrity [CITE:ev_2852c4416e3e0b87]. Adhesion performance scoring scales vary significantly between qualitative visual assessments and quantitative force measurements, influencing the reliability of data obtained for different substrate and coating combinations [CITE:ev_84a08742318ed15d]. Consequently, the choice of method determines whether the resulting data supports pass/fail screening or engineering specification validation [CITE:ev_be43a72512267deb].\n\nThe cross-cut test method remains a predominant technique for qualitative evaluation, particularly for thin polymeric coatings on rigid substrates [CITE:ev_067cc0abd42529d2]. This method involves cutting a lattice pattern of small squares into the coating down to the substrate [CITE:ev_1962a4977f7b146f]. Evaluation is conducted by applying pressure-sensitive tape over the lattice and removing it to assess coating removal [CITE:ev_815bc543dbac8f04]. The results are classified into six levels according to the degree of peeling observed after tape removal [CITE:ev_cd6ffc422440dd18]. The evaluation ranges from 0B, representing complete detachment, to 5B, representing no peeling or detachment [CITE:ev_d0d69c4b450fa1e3]. Alternative scoring classifications utilize a 0A to 5A scale, where 5A indicates no peeling and 0A indicates complete removal [CITE:ev_5311f47cd26b20c1]. Results can also be rated on a scale from 0\u20135 indicating adhesion quality [CITE:ev_cb4b51f1d8395d4c]. This standardization allows for consistent reporting under ASTM D3359, which was codified in 1974 [CITE:ev_cee97c994fefc9b1]. The cross hatch tape test is primarily intended for use in the laboratory on coatings less than 5 mils (125 microns) thick [CITE:ev_c8b75c8d91e7faea]. The tape test is inherently qualitative and cannot precisely assess quantitatively the actual strength of the adhesion [CITE:ev_389d441cc3fe3ba4]. High scores of 4-5 suggest poor adhesion which may lead to premature paint failure [CITE:ev_a803b922378c99e8]. Independent laboratories have noted differences of up to 50 percent in tape adhesion variability over time, suggesting that qualitative methods may suffer from reproducibility issues [CITE:ev_ce3124033536bdb1].\n\nIn contrast, pull-off adhesion testing provides quantitative evaluation of coating adhesion, measuring the strength of the bond between a coating and the surface it is applied to [CITE:ev_d62d99d84a009e00]. This method applies tensile force perpendicular to the substrate to measure coating resistance to separation [CITE:ev_057ad7d7fadf39c2]. The resulting force is measured in MPa or psi, revealing the coating's tensile pull-off strength [CITE:ev_11ae17d7fc3f76c6]. Standards such as ASTM D4541 and ASTM D7234 define the procedures for measuring pull-off strength using portable adhesion testers [CITE:ev_3a7d481c74614432]. The ISO 4624 standard is also referenced as a global standard for pull-off adhesion testing compliance [CITE:ev_a9eaff2972823a42]. The pull-off test is standardized by ISO 4624:2023 and is widely used to quantify adhesion [CITE:ev_7267f03f92de9267]. For the test to be considered valid, the coating must cover at least half of the surface of the stamps [CITE:ev_d0994f38fa60bebe]. Despite its quantitative nature, there is significant disagreement in the literature regarding the fundamental validity of the data produced [CITE:ev_1a095db8b38db71d]. One perspective asserts that the pull-off adhesion test measures the strength of the bond between a coating and the surface [CITE:ev_d62d99d84a009e00]. Conversely, conflicting evidence suggests that the test method does not measure fundamental adhesion strength of the bond between coating and substrate [CITE:ev_1a095db8b38db71d]. Given that the latter claim is supported by evidence while the former is unverified, it must be acknowledged that pull-off values may represent a system strength influenced by cohesive properties rather than pure interfacial adhesion. This distinction is vital when comparing epoxy, urethane, and silicone materials, as cohesive failure within the coating may yield high pull-off values despite poor interfacial bonding. For example, the adhesion pull-off strength of a polyaspartic coating published in its data sheet is 900 psi [CITE:ev_c79263c59377e969]. However, industrial-grade adhesives typically have tensile strength ranging from 10 MPa to over 60 MPa depending on formulation [CITE:ev_a85f361a2bdff124].\n\nPeel testing offers a third evaluation pathway, measuring adhesive strength by pulling one material away from another at a specific angle and speed [CITE:ev_f0f1594bc5d9dd20]. Key peel test metrics include initial peak force, average force across the entire seal, and peel strength [CITE:ev_9ae74d816a7eacc6]. This method is particularly relevant for flexible coatings or adhesives where tensile pull-off might induce premature cohesive failure. For silicone materials, results showed little dependence on peel speed, but low adhesion strength of silicones was apparent [CITE:ev_1377196d9ff7c638]. Furthermore, cure improves adhesion except for silicone, where failure occurred at the tape interface [CITE:ev_0db5383239a26b95]. This suggests that scoring scales for silicone must account for interface-specific failure modes that differ from the cohesive tearing observed in epoxy systems. Indeed, good agreement was found between hot water-adhesion tests and TxDOT peel tests for epoxy coatings [CITE:ev_6c07ce38730c09f8], indicating that peel metrics may correlate well with environmental resistance for specific polymer types. Scrape test adhesion is measured in kilograms of force required for removal, offering another quantitative metric for hard coatings [CITE:ev_73f6c6e661c92e30]. Using a calibrated knife reduced subjectivity found in other adhesion tests, providing a middle ground between the qualitative cross-cut and the quantitative pull-off [CITE:ev_5caae816b34f9fb7]. The test method is commonly used for coating thicknesses in the range of 0.1 to 30 micrometers [CITE:ev_8858c4a9dba538b2].\n\nTable 10.1 compares the primary adhesion testing methods, their associated standards, measurement units, and scoring scales based on the available evidence.\n\n| Test Method | Primary Standards | Measurement Unit | Scoring Scale | Evaluation Nature |\n| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |\n| Cross-Cut / Tape | ASTM D3359, ISO 2409, JIS K 5600-5-6 | N/A (Visual) | 0B (Complete Detachment) to 5B (No Peeling) [CITE:ev_d0d69c4b450fa1e3] | Qualitative [CITE:ev_84a08742318ed15d] |\n| Pull-Off | ASTM D4541, ASTM D7234, ISO 4624 | MPa or psi [CITE:ev_11ae17d7fc3f76c6] | Continuous Numerical Value (e.g., 900 psi) [CITE:ev_c79263c59377e969] | Quantitative [CITE:ev_84a08742318ed15d] |\n| Peel Test | ASTM D903, ISO 11339, JIS Z0237 | Force (N/mm or similar) | Initial Peak Force, Average Force [CITE:ev_9ae74d816a7eacc6] | Quantitative |\n| Knife / Scrape | ASTM D6677 | Kilograms of force [CITE:ev_73f6c6e661c92e30] | Force Required for Removal | Quantitative / Semi-Quantitative |\n\nThe table highlights the trade-offs between methods. Cross-cut testing, while standardized under ISO 2409 for sheet metal parts [CITE:ev_067cc0abd42529d2], provides only qualitative data suitable for pass/fail criteria [CITE:ev_cb4b51f1d8395d4c]. A score of 0 indicates no paint removal representing excellent adhesion performance [CITE:ev_cb4b51f1d8395d4c]. Conversely, pull-off methods provide values for peel stress allowing quick determination and comparison of coating adhesion [CITE:ev_03d5cf9642fafd22]. Adhesion pull-off strength testing determines the overall quality and integrity of an installed coating system on concrete, metal, wood, and rigid substrates [CITE:ev_c357968c8f3b130a].\n\nMaterial-specific performance further complicates the selection of scoring criteria. For polyurethane-based coatings filled with silicone rubber nanoparticles, the cross-cut tape peel test was used to evaluate the adhesion property [CITE:ev_1962a4977f7b146f]. However, untreated PA showed lowest work of adhesion at 69.7 mN/m with highest interfacial tension leading to weak coating retention [CITE:ev_96b127d3014b3201]. This indicates that surface energy measurements could serve as complementary methods to traditional cross-cut tape tests [CITE:ev_d77bb9a950ab7132]. For silicone roof coatings, adhesion test is recommended to ensure sufficient adhesion between substrate and fluid-applied roof coatings [CITE:ev_14eab9a16ceed3db]. Yet, traditional adhesion tests like ISO/JIS cross-hatch and ASTM tape tests only evaluate final adhesion strength without explaining adhesion mechanisms [CITE:ev_60e99e00ac021116]. This limitation is critical when assessing urethane adhesives, where comprehensive adhesive suitability requires comparing peel adhesion, tensile strength, and shear resistance [CITE:ev_ac671c6a322fe8bd].\n\nThe implications of these scoring differences extend to regulatory compliance and warranty validation. Failure to verify and document adhesion results may result in adhesion loss not covered by Dow Warranty [CITE:ev_a889a20cea5550f0]. Site adhesion testing must be verified according to criteria to confirm proper surface preparation [CITE:ev_97840fd9469c02ca]. Field adhesion tests, also called hand pull tests, verify proper sealant installation [CITE:ev_7658ca702fb6e4fa]. However, field adhesion tests must be performed at the shop or jobsite to verify sealant adhesion, introducing potential environmental variables not present in laboratory settings [CITE:ev_5da5d464178b2eea]. Adhesion variance results from surface treatment or the nature of the substrate, necessitating that scoring criteria account for substrate-specific baselines [CITE:ev_eb6a7ba09f91b0a1].\n\nWhen evaluating epoxy coatings, coating application quality varies by coater and impacts adhesion [CITE:ev_315057e2cc7dd3f8]. Therefore, scoring scales must be robust enough to detect application inconsistencies. Adhesion strength of coating to substrate defines overall coating integrity and performance [CITE:ev_2852c4416e3e0b87]. Evaluating adhesion before and after exposure lengths helps estimate adhesion retention [CITE:ev_02597b3988622977]. This is particularly relevant for environmental testing where temperature and immersion effects degrade the bond over time. While the pull-off test is standardized by ISO 4624:2023 and is widely used to quantify adhesion [CITE:ev_7267f03f92de9267], the ISO 4624 standard is designed primarily for laboratory adhesion testing applications [CITE:ev_1cb5bc8d295f5d28]. This restricts the direct comparison of field data obtained via ASTM D7234 on concrete with laboratory data obtained via ISO 4624 on metal [CITE:ev_3a7d481c74614432].\n\nUltimately, the selection of a scoring scale depends on the required precision and the material system. Adhesive failure indicates molecular attraction between adhesive and substrate was weaker than internal cohesion [CITE:ev_1826db32940b1bde]. Mechanical adhesion occurs when adhesive penetrates microscopic ridges and valleys of substrate anchoring the coating [CITE:ev_1826db32940b1bde]. Scoring methods that cannot distinguish between these failure modes, such as the qualitative cross-cut, obscure the root cause of adhesion loss. Comprehensive adhesive suitability requires comparing peel adhesion, tensile strength, and shear resistance to fully characterize the bond [CITE:ev_ac671c6a322fe8bd]. For silicone systems, where low adhesion strength is apparent, reliance on high-threshold quantitative scales like pull-off result in consistent failure ratings, whereas peel tests offer better resolution of the weak boundary layer [CITE:ev_1377196d9ff7c638]. Conversely, for high-strength epoxy systems, the qualitative 5B rating of cross-cut tests saturate, providing no differentiation between excellent and superior performance, necessitating the use of MPa-based pull-off metrics [CITE:ev_11ae17d7fc3f76c6].\n\n**Key Findings**\n\n*   Cross-cut testing utilizes a 0B to 5B scoring scale where 5B indicates no peeling, but remains qualitative and cannot precisely assess quantitative strength [CITE:ev_d0d69c4b450fa1e3], [CITE:ev_389d441cc3fe3ba4].\n*   Pull-off testing provides quantitative data in MPa or psi but evidence conflicts on whether it measures fundamental bond strength or system strength influenced by cohesive properties [CITE:ev_11ae17d7fc3f76c6], [CITE:ev_1a095db8b38db71d].\n*   Silicone materials exhibit low adhesion strength in peel tests with failure often occurring at the tape interface, requiring specific evaluation criteria distinct from epoxy or urethane systems [CITE:ev_1377196d9ff7c638], [CITE:ev_0db5383239a26b95].\n*   Field adhesion testing introduces variability compared to laboratory standards like ISO 4624, necessitating verified criteria to ensure warranty compliance and proper surface preparation confirmation [CITE:ev_5da5d464178b2eea], [CITE:ev_a889a20cea5550f0].\n*   Comprehensive evaluation of polymer coatings requires comparing multiple metrics including peel adhesion, tensile strength, and shear resistance rather than relying on a single scoring scale [CITE:ev_ac671c6a322fe8bd].\n\n![Numeric Values by Measurement Label](data:image/png;base64,)\n*Figure 1: Bar chart showing numeric values extracted from measurement and statistic labels.*\n\n\n\n![Timeline of Standards and Document Metrics](data:image/png;base64,)\n*Figure 2: Line chart tracking years associated with standards and document metadata.*\n\n\n\n| Data Type | Count |\n| --- | --- |\n| measurement | 3 |\n| statistic | 6 |\n| time_series | 5 |\n\n*Table 1: Summary of Data Types in Dataset*\n\n\n## Peel Testing Standards ASTM D903 and ISO11339 Compliance\n\nPeel testing standards such as ASTM D903 and ISO 11339 provide the structural basis for quantifying adhesive strength in flexible coating systems, distinguishing them from qualitative alternatives. ASTM D903-98 specifically governs peel adhesion testing conducted at a 180-degree angle [CITE:ev_23793fa90d97bbac]. Similarly, ISO 11339:1990 establishes the protocol for 180-degree peel tests, while ISO 11339:1993 is applied to T-peel testing on aluminium alloys [CITE:ev_10c49ae89ab921be][CITE:ev_f93ca08a9dc0966f]. These standards differ fundamentally from cross-cut and pull-off methods, which serve distinct evaluative purposes within the regulatory landscape. Peel tests measure adhesive strength by pulling one material away from another at a specific angle and speed [CITE:ev_f0f1594bc5d9dd20]. In contrast, the Cross-Cut Test provides qualitative evaluation while the Pull-Off Method provides quantitative evaluation of coating adhesion [CITE:ev_84a08742318ed15d]. This distinction is critical for regulatory compliance, as quantitative data is often required for safety submissions.\n\nTo contextualize these methods within the research question regarding epoxy, urethane, and silicone materials, the following table compares the operational parameters of the primary adhesion testing standards.\n\n| Test Method | Primary Standard | Configuration | Measurement Type | Substrate Suitability | Thickness Limit | Units |\n| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |\n| Peel Test | ASTM D903 / ISO 11339 | 180-degree or T-Peel | Quantitative (Force/Width) | Flexible coatings [CITE:ev_89558a7b16b51284] | N/A | N/mm or lb/in [CITE:ev_44fea46094d83d2a] |\n| Cross-Cut | ASTM D3359 / ISO 2409 | Lattice Pattern | Qualitative (0B-5B) | Metallic substrates [CITE:ev_949116df55b626a8] | 250 micrometers [CITE:ev_a256fbf9c6062207] | Classification Scale [CITE:ev_d0d69c4b450fa1e3] |\n| Pull-Off | ASTM D4541 / ISO 4624 | Perpendicular Tensile | Quantitative (Stress) | Concrete and Metal [CITE:ev_3a7d481c74614432] | N/A | MPa [CITE:ev_5bd33c7a02d9cd28] |\n| Tape Test | ASTM D3359 Method B | Pressure-Sensitive Tape | Qualitative | Metal or Plastic [CITE:ev_bb2ddf4927283db1] | 5 mils [CITE:ev_c8b75c8d91e7faea] | 0A-5A Scale [CITE:ev_5311f47cd26b20c1] |\n\nThe data indicates that peel testing offers a quantitative advantage over cross-cut methods, which are limited by coating thickness. The Cross-Cut Test is applicable for layer thicknesses up to about 250 \u00b5m [CITE:ev_df003953c7f7a2d0]. Peel strength is calculated using force applied during peeling process divided by film width [CITE:ev_1ae4530e37be2c91]. This calculation yields units such as N/mm or lb/in, allowing for precise comparison across different polymer coatings [CITE:ev_44fea46094d83d2a]. For silicone materials, results showed little dependence on peel speed, but low adhesion strength of silicones was apparent [CITE:ev_1377196d9ff7c638]. This finding shows that while peel testing is applicable to silicones, the absolute strength values are lower compared to epoxy or urethane systems. Results showed good agreement between hot water-adhesion tests and TxDOT peel tests, indicating reliability for epoxy coatings [CITE:ev_6c07ce38730c09f8].\n\nEnvironmental control is necessary because peel test values represent an average between 5 and 35mm of peel for each of two tests [CITE:ev_e84426d0da8ec986]. Variations in test parameters can influence outcomes; for instance, peel speed was varied from 0.5 to 1.5mm/s to test effects on three coatings [CITE:ev_bd20a3afb1754c5c]. Despite these controls, conflicts exist regarding specimen preparation dimensions. One evidence piece states peel test uses 1 inch wide by 6 inch long fabric strip embedded in coating base layer [CITE:ev_14c32495c2f3b455]. However, another source indicates DOWSIL ALLGUARD coating adhesion test uses cheesecloth strip 1 inch by 12 inches embedded in wet coating [CITE:ev_234e921351755d24]. This discrepancy highlights a lack of uniformity in strip dimensions across different industry applications, potentially affecting comparability between ASTM D903 compliance and specific manufacturer protocols.\n\nRegulatory codification dates further illustrate the evolution of these standards. ASTM D3359 Standard Test Method for Measuring Adhesion by Tape Test was codified in 1974 [CITE:ev_cee97c994fefc9b1]. In comparison, pull-off testing follows ISO 4624, ASTM D4541, and D7234 standards for evaluating coating peel strength [CITE:ev_0ffc84130522e5ce]. The ISO 4624 standard is designed primarily for laboratory adhesion testing applications [CITE:ev_1cb5bc8d295f5d28]. International variations also exist; JIS K 5600-5-6: 1999 specifies the Cross-Cut Test method for coating adhesion evaluation under Japanese Industrial Standards [CITE:ev_38e4b21684c9f29c]. Meanwhile, JIS K 5600-5-7: 2014 specifies the pull-off method as coating tensile adhesion test for quantitative evaluation [CITE:ev_389eb7da7cde2ff8]. This progression from cross-cut to pull-off in Japanese standards mirrors the global shift towards quantitative data for regulatory compliance.\n\nA significant theoretical conflict exists regarding what these tests actually measure. One source states pull-off adhesion test measures the strength of the bond between a coating and the surface [CITE:ev_d62d99d84a009e00]. Conversely, another evidence piece claims test method does not measure fundamental adhesion strength of the bond between coating and substrate [CITE:ev_1a095db8b38db71d]. This disagreement shows that while pull-off and peel tests provide quantitative numbers, they may not isolate the fundamental molecular adhesion from cohesive failure within the coating. Traditional adhesion tests like ISO/JIS cross-hatch and ASTM tape tests only evaluate final adhesion strength without explaining adhesion mechanisms [CITE:ev_60e99e00ac021116]. Therefore, comprehensive adhesive suitability requires comparing peel adhesion, tensile strength, and shear resistance [CITE:ev_ac671c6a322fe8bd]. Relying solely on one method obscures the true performance of urethane or epoxy coatings under stress.\n\nMarket expectations further define compliance thresholds. Market expectations for removable adhesives specify 1.75 pli peel strength after 3 weeks at 70C [CITE:ev_074ce7c9909d0868]. This specific metric demonstrates how peel testing standards are adapted for commercial warranty requirements beyond laboratory conditions. Peel-back and pull-off data should not be compared since testing mechanisms differ [CITE:ev_aabe5f967b113f45]. This limitation is crucial for the research question, as comparing epoxy pull-off data directly with silicone peel data would yield invalid conclusions regarding relative performance. Peel testing is suitable for designs where bonded edges experience prying or bending [CITE:ev_1be768df70c46a07]. Thus, for polymer coatings on metal roofing or flexible substrates, peel standards like ASTM D903 are more relevant than cross-cut methods intended for rigid substrates.\n\nCross-cut test is especially suitable for thin layers on rigid substrates like metal or plastic [CITE:ev_bb2ddf4927283db1]. However, ASTM D3359 procedures assess the adhesion of coating films specifically on metallic substrates [CITE:ev_949116df55b626a8]. This restricts the utility of cross-cut testing for non-metallic applications common in urethane and silicone coatings. The Cross-Cut Test classifies adhesion results into six levels according to the degree of peeling after tape removal [CITE:ev_cd6ffc422440dd18]. These levels range from 0B representing complete detachment to 5B representing no detachment [CITE:ev_d0d69c4b450fa1e3]. Such qualitative scales lack the precision required for high-performance regulatory submissions where force per width metrics are mandated. Pull-off test data provides quantitative indication of bond tensile strength [CITE:ev_448284e198da9b99]. Consequently, while cross-cut tests remain common for quality assurance, peel and pull-off methods are preferred for compliance documentation.\n\nSite adhesion testing must be verified according to criteria on page 35 to confirm proper surface preparation procedures [CITE:ev_97840fd9469c02ca]. This requirement underscores that standard compliance is not merely about the test execution but also about preconditioning. ASTM Committee D01 standards requiring tape for adhesion testing are under ballot review [CITE:ev_dc3bcca2950ab7fe]. This ongoing review indicates that even established standards like ASTM D3359 are subject to revision based on new performance data. For the comparative analysis of polymer coatings, this implies that reliance on older tape test standards may not reflect current best practices for epoxy or urethane systems. Hydroxyapatite coatings showed repeatable adhesion strength of 3.4 \u00b1 1.0 MPa across both ASTM test methods, demonstrating that quantitative consistency is achievable with proper standardization [CITE:ev_5bd33c7a02d9cd28].\n\nThe Chinese national standard GB/T 2790-1995 specifies the 180\u00b0 peel strength test method steps and requirements [CITE:ev_4ec63e8773cfcd3e]. Global harmonization of such standards facilitates the comparison of coating performance across different regulatory regions. Nitto Denko employs adhesive pull strength evaluation using JIS Z0237 and ASTM standards [CITE:ev_d063340009e4394c]. This multi-standard approach ensures compatibility with both domestic and international markets. However, the tape test is qualitative and cannot precisely assess quantitatively the actual strength of the adhesion [CITE:ev_389d441cc3fe3ba4]. Independent laboratories have noted differences of up to 50 percent in tape adhesion variability over time [CITE:ev_ce3124033536bdb1]. This high variability further supports the preference for peel testing standards like ASTM D903 when quantitative precision is required for the research question.\n\nIn summary, ASTM D903 and ISO 11339 provide the necessary framework for quantitative peel testing, distinguishing them from the qualitative limitations of cross-cut methods. While conflicts exist regarding specimen dimensions and the fundamental nature of the measurement, the consensus favors quantitative methods for regulatory compliance. The evidence shows that for silicone materials, peel strength is lower but measurable, whereas epoxy systems show strong correlation with peel test results. Urethane materials, while less explicitly detailed in the evidence, fall under the general coating standards applicable to metallic and rigid substrates. The divergence in standards codification dates and ongoing ballot reviews indicate a dynamic regulatory environment that prioritizes quantitative data for safety and warranty verification.\n\n**Key Findings**\n*   ASTM D903-98 and ISO 11339:1990 govern 180-degree peel testing, providing quantitative force/width metrics unlike qualitative cross-cut methods [CITE:ev_23793fa90d97bbac][CITE:ev_10c49ae89ab921be][CITE:ev_84a08742318ed15d].\n*   Cross-cut testing is limited to coating thicknesses up to 250 micrometers and metallic substrates, restricting its use for thicker polymer coatings [CITE:ev_a256fbf9c6062207][CITE:ev_949116df55b626a8].\n*   Conflicting evidence exists regarding peel strip dimensions (1x6 inches vs 1x12 inches) and whether tests measure fundamental bond strength, requiring careful method selection [CITE:ev_14c32495c2f3b455][CITE:ev_234e921351755d24][CITE:ev_1a095db8b38db71d].\n*   Silicone materials exhibit low adhesion strength in peel tests with little dependence on peel speed, while epoxy coatings show good agreement with peel test metrics [CITE:ev_1377196d9ff7c638][CITE:ev_6c07ce38730c09f8].\n*   Regulatory compliance increasingly favors quantitative data, driving the shift from tape tests to peel and pull-off standards due to tape test variability of up to 50 percent [CITE:ev_389d441cc3fe3ba4][CITE:ev_ce3124033536bdb1].\n\n![Measurement Specifications](data:image/png;base64,: .\codex_tmp_billing_quota_store_review_alt: Access is denied.    1: # Carney demo runbook — operator playbook
   2: 
   3: This is the single source of truth for the PM Mark Carney POLARIS demo. Read end-to-end before the meeting; refer back during.
   4: 
   5: **Demo target window:** 2026-06-05 to 2026-06-09. Updated 2026-05-13 after all I-arch-001* + I-carney-002..005 PRs merged.
   6: 
   7: ---
   8: 
   9: ## §0 — Prereqs (1 week before demo)
  10: 
  11: | Item | Owner | Status |
  12: |---|---|---|
  13: | AWS account with ca-central-1 IAM admin | Ops | (verify) |
  14: | Route 53 public hosted zone | Ops | (verify) |
  15: | `terraform` + `gh` + `aws` CLIs installed | Ops | `terraform --version && gh --version && aws --version` |
  16: | Demo signing GPG key generated | Ops | `bash scripts/bootstrap_gpg_demo_key.sh` |
  17: | OpenRouter + Serper API keys procured | Ops | private records |

  19: | Fallback laptop ready | Lead | `docker compose -f docker-compose.v6.yml up -d` on laptop |
  20: 
  21: ## §1 — Deploy day-1 (T-7 before demo)
  22: 
  23: ```bash
  24: cd infra/aws
  25: cp terraform.tfvars.example terraform.tfvars
  26: # Edit terraform.tfvars: domain_name, route53_zone_name, polaris_repo_commit
  27: # (current polaris HEAD SHA), openrouter/serper keys, polaris_gpg_key_id,
  28: # polaris_gpg_pubkey, static_accounts_yaml (bcrypt hashes), gpg_private_key_armored.
  29: 
  30: aws s3 mb s3://polaris-carney-tf-state --region ca-central-1
  31: aws s3api put-bucket-versioning \
  32:     --bucket polaris-carney-tf-state \
  33:     --versioning-configuration Status=Enabled
  34: 
  35: terraform init \
  36:     -backend-config="bucket=polaris-carney-tf-state" \
  37:     -backend-config="region=ca-central-1" \
  38:     -backend-config="key=polaris-carney/terraform.tfstate"
  39: 
  40: terraform plan -out plan.bin
  41: terraform apply plan.bin
  42: ```
  43: 
  44: Wait ~10 minutes for EC2 + ACM + cloud-init. Verify:
  45: 
  46: ```bash
  47: curl -fsS https://polaris.<your-domain>/health
  48: curl -fsS https://polaris.<your-domain>/transparency | jq
  49: ```
  50: 
  51: ### §1b — Install egress lockdown (mandatory before §2 smoke test)
  52: 
  53: Per Codex iter-1 P1-2: cloud-init does NOT auto-run `egress_lockdown.sh` because the first compose build needs pypi/npmjs/debian access. After the first `docker compose up -d` succeeds inside cloud-init, SSM into the host and install the lockdown:
  54: 
  55: ```bash
  56: INSTANCE_ID=$(cd infra/aws && terraform output -raw ec2_instance_id)
  57: aws ssm start-session --target $INSTANCE_ID --region ca-central-1
  58: # Inside the SSM session:
  59: sudo bash /opt/polaris/scripts/egress_lockdown.sh
  60: sudo iptables -L POLARIS_EGRESS_HOST -n -v | head -20
  61: sudo iptables -L POLARIS_EGRESS_DOCKER -n -v | head -20
  62: ```
  63: 
  64: Both chains must show DROP rules at the bottom + the allowlisted IPs as ACCEPT. Without this step, the deploy claims egress controls but they are NOT enforced — `/transparency`'s `enforcement_layer` field would be inaccurate.
  65: 
  66: ## §2 — Smoke test (T-3 before demo)
  67: 
  68: Authenticate, submit a clinical question, verify the bundle round-trips:
  69: 
  70: ```bash
  71: TOKEN=$(curl -fsS -X POST https://polaris.<your-domain>/api/v6/auth/login \
  72:     -H 'content-type: application/json' \
  73:     -d '{"username":"carney_office","password":"<reviewer-pw>"}' \
  74:     | jq -r .access_token)
  75: 
  76: # Submit a run.
  77: RUN_ID=$(curl -fsS -X POST https://polaris.<your-domain>/api/v6/runs \
  78:     -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  79:     -H 'content-type: application/json' \
  80:     -d '{"template":"clinical","question":"Is tirzepatide effective for type 2 diabetes?"}' \
  81:     | jq -r .run_id)
  82: echo "Run: $RUN_ID"
  83: 
  84: # Tail the event stream.
  85: curl -N -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  86:     https://polaris.<your-domain>/api/v6/stream/$RUN_ID
  87: 
  88: # Fetch the signed bundle.
  89: curl -fsS -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  90:     -o bundle.tar.gz \
  91:     https://polaris.<your-domain>/api/v6/runs/$RUN_ID/bundle.tar.gz
  92: tar -xzf bundle.tar.gz
  93: 
  94: # Verify the GPG signature from the operator workstation (NOT from inside
  95: # the deploy — the whole point is independent verification).
  96: cd audit_*/
  97: curl -fsS https://polaris.<your-domain>/transparency/pubkey.asc | gpg --import
  98: gpg --verify manifest.yaml.asc manifest.yaml
  99: ```
 100: 
 101: Expect: `Good signature from "POLARIS Carney Demo <signing@polaris.local>"`.
 102:

 118: ## §4 — Live demo (T=0)
 119: 
 120: 1. Open https://polaris.<your-domain>/ in browser, log in as `carney_office`.
 121: 2. Show the home / intake screen.
 122: 3. Submit Q1 (tirzepatide) — narrate the scope gate + retrieval + verification phases as SSE events stream.
 123: 4. When the run completes, click into the Inspector — show source-tier mix, verified-sentence Inspector pane.
 124: 5. Download the bundle — verify the signature on operator laptop (NOT in browser; demo independence).
 125: 6. Navigate to `/transparency` — read aloud the sovereignty filter, evaluator models, egress allowlist. Hand Carney's office the URL.
 126: 7. Submit Q2 (pharmacare) — observe sovereignty cascade in real time.
 127: 8. Take questions.
 128: 
 129: ## §5 — Fallback laptop
 130: 
 131: If the AWS deploy is unreachable (DNS, ALB down, etc.) during the demo:
 132: 
 133: **One-time setup (T-3 before demo):** populate the laptop's `.env` + `/etc/polaris/static_accounts.yaml` from AWS Secrets Manager so the laptop matches the production substrate.
 134: 
 135: ```bash
 136: cd /local/polaris
 137: 
 138: # 1. Pull secrets from AWS Secrets Manager to the laptop.
 139: sm() { aws secretsmanager get-secret-value --secret-id "$1" --query SecretString --output text --region ca-central-1; }
 140: 
 141: cat > .env <<EOF
 142: OPENROUTER_API_KEY=$(sm /polaris/v6/openrouter_api_key 2>/dev/null || cat ~/.polaris/openrouter_api_key)
 143: SERPER_API_KEY=$(sm /polaris/v6/serper_api_key 2>/dev/null || cat ~/.polaris/serper_api_key)
 144: POLARIS_JWT_SECRET=$(sm polaris/v6/jwt_secret)
 145: POLARIS_GPG_KEY_ID=$(sm /polaris/v6/polaris_gpg_key_id)
 146: POLARIS_GPG_HOMEDIR=$HOME/.gnupg-polaris
 147: POLARIS_STATIC_ACCOUNTS_PATH=/etc/polaris/static_accounts.yaml
 148: POLARIS_ETC_DIR=/etc/polaris
 149: POLARIS_API_PORT=8000
 150: POLARIS_WEB_PORT=3000
 151: PG_MAX_COST_PER_RUN=5.00
 152: EOF
 153: chmod 600 .env
 154: 
 155: # 2. Write static_accounts.yaml to /etc/polaris (mirroring EC2 host path).
 156: sudo mkdir -p /etc/polaris
 157: sudo chmod 750 /etc/polaris
 158: sudo bash -c "sm polaris/v6/static_accounts_yaml > /etc/polaris/static_accounts.yaml"
 159: sudo chmod 640 /etc/polaris/static_accounts.yaml
 160: 
 161: # 3. Import the GPG private key into the local keyring.
 162: sm polaris/v6/gpg_private_key_armored | gpg --homedir ~/.gnupg-polaris --batch --import
 163: 
 164: # 4. Bring up the stack.
 165: docker compose -f docker-compose.v6.yml down
 166: docker compose -f docker-compose.v6.yml up -d --build
 167: 
 168: # 5. Smoke test.
 169: curl -fsS http://localhost:8000/health
 170: curl -fsS http://localhost:8000/transparency | jq
 171: ```
 172: 
 173: Localhost demo at http://localhost:3000. Auth + signing + sovereignty filter all behave identically to the AWS deploy since the laptop loads the SAME secrets. /transparency will show `region: "unknown"` (no AWS_REGION env) — disclose this if a reviewer asks why.
 174: 
 175: ## §6 — 30-minute internal rehearsal (T-1 before demo)
 176: 
 177: Schedule a 30-min Zoom with at least one non-engineer:
 178: - They follow the demo script as the "Carney's office" surrogate.
 179: - Time each step. Target: full demo < 20 min (10 min buffer for questions).
 180: - Verify the bundle GPG verify on their workstation (not yours).
 181: - Verify the /transparency page is readable.
 182: - Pad any unclear narration.
 183: 
 184: ## §7 — Codex sign-off (T-1 before demo)
 185: 
 186: Per Codex iter-1 P1-1: the sign-off must NOT pipe the static brief alone (that lets Codex see placeholders, not real evidence). Instead, copy the brief to a working file, RUN each curl/GPG/iptables command, and PASTE the actual outputs into the working file BEFORE shipping to Codex.
 187: 
 188: ```bash
 189: # Copy brief to evidence file.
 190: cp .codex/I-carney-007/carney_demo_signoff_brief.md \
 191:    /tmp/carney_demo_signoff_evidence.md
 192: 
 193: # Run each §3 command and append its output. Example for §3a:
 194: echo "" >> /tmp/carney_demo_signoff_evidence.md
 195: echo "## §3a evidence (live transparency response):" >> /tmp/carney_demo_signoff_evidence.md
 196: echo '```' >> /tmp/carney_demo_signoff_evidence.md
 197: curl -fsS https://polaris.<your-domain>/transparency \
 198:     >> /tmp/carney_demo_signoff_evidence.md
 199: echo '```' >> /tmp/carney_demo_signoff_evidence.md
 200: 
 201: # Repeat for §3b /health, §3c bundle Q1 GPG verify, §3d bundle Q2 GPG verify,
 202: # §4 egress chain output. Then submit to Codex:
 203: env -u OPENAI_API_KEY codex exec --skip-git-repo-check - < /tmp/carney_demo_signoff_evidence.md
 204: ```
 205: 
 206: If the evidence file still contains `<your-domain>` or other placeholders, ABORT — Codex would otherwise sign off on the example template instead of the real deploy. Expected verdict: `verdict: APPROVE ship_decision: SHIP convergence_call: accept_remaining`. Anything REQUEST_CHANGES or `ship_decision: HALT` → escalate to I-carney-006 (live rehearsal) for the failing question.
 207: 
 208: ## §8 — Post-demo tear-down
 209: 
 210: ```bash
 211: # Recover audit bundles BEFORE destroy.
 212: aws s3 sync s3://polaris-carney-audit-<acct-id>/ ./carney_demo_bundles/
 213: 
 214: # Tear down the AWS stack.
 215: cd infra/aws
 216: terraform destroy
 217: ```

 221: - Single-AZ EC2 (no HA pair) — for demo window only

 223: - Pipeline-A only (pipeline-B legacy + pipeline-C frozen per architecture.md §5)
 224: - 18-domain egress allowlist enforced via iptables (not VPC NACLs — also defense-in-depth Phase-2)
 225: - /docs + /redoc + /openapi.json public for operator ergonomics — gate to admin in Phase-2

 180: +# Carney demo runbook — operator playbook
 181: +
 182: +This is the single source of truth for the PM Mark Carney POLARIS demo. Read end-to-end before the meeting; refer back during.
 183: +
 184: +**Demo target window:** 2026-06-05 to 2026-06-09. Updated 2026-05-13 after all I-arch-001* + I-carney-002..005 PRs merged.
 185: +
 186: +---
 187: +
 188: +## §0 — Prereqs (1 week before demo)
 189: +
 190: +| Item | Owner | Status |
 191: +|---|---|---|
 192: +| AWS account with ca-central-1 IAM admin | Ops | (verify) |
 193: +| Route 53 public hosted zone | Ops | (verify) |
 194: +| `terraform` + `gh` + `aws` CLIs installed | Ops | `terraform --version && gh --version && aws --version` |
 195: +| Demo signing GPG key generated | Ops | `bash scripts/bootstrap_gpg_demo_key.sh` |
 196: +| OpenRouter + Serper API keys procured | Ops | private records |
 197: +| Carney office contact + demo time confirmed | Lead | calendar invite |
 198: +| Fallback laptop ready | Lead | `docker compose -f docker-compose.v6.yml up -d` on laptop |
 199: +
 200: +## §1 — Deploy day-1 (T-7 before demo)
 201: +
 202: +```bash
 203: +cd infra/aws
 204: +cp terraform.tfvars.example terraform.tfvars
 205: +# Edit terraform.tfvars: domain_name, route53_zone_name, polaris_repo_commit
 206: +# (current polaris HEAD SHA), openrouter/serper keys, polaris_gpg_key_id,
 207: +# polaris_gpg_pubkey, static_accounts_yaml (bcrypt hashes), gpg_private_key_armored.
 208: +
 209: +aws s3 mb s3://polaris-carney-tf-state --region ca-central-1
 210: +aws s3api put-bucket-versioning \
 211: +    --bucket polaris-carney-tf-state \
 212: +    --versioning-configuration Status=Enabled
 213: +
 214: +terraform init \
 215: +    -backend-config="bucket=polaris-carney-tf-state" \
 216: +    -backend-config="region=ca-central-1" \
 217: +    -backend-config="key=polaris-carney/terraform.tfstate"
 218: +
 219: +terraform plan -out plan.bin
 220: +terraform apply plan.bin
 221: +```
 222: +
 223: +Wait ~10 minutes for EC2 + ACM + cloud-init. Verify:
 224: +
 225: +```bash
 226: +curl -fsS https://polaris.<your-domain>/health
 227: +curl -fsS https://polaris.<your-domain>/transparency | jq
 228: +```
 229: +
 230: +### §1b — Install egress lockdown (mandatory before §2 smoke test)
 231: +
 232: +Per Codex iter-1 P1-2: cloud-init does NOT auto-run `egress_lockdown.sh` because the first compose build needs pypi/npmjs/debian access. After the first `docker compose up -d` succeeds inside cloud-init, SSM into the host and install the lockdown:
 233: +
 234: +```bash
 235: +INSTANCE_ID=$(cd infra/aws && terraform output -raw ec2_instance_id)
 236: +aws ssm start-session --target $INSTANCE_ID --region ca-central-1
 237: +# Inside the SSM session:
 238: +sudo bash /opt/polaris/scripts/egress_lockdown.sh
 239: +sudo iptables -L POLARIS_EGRESS_HOST -n -v | head -20
 240: +sudo iptables -L POLARIS_EGRESS_DOCKER -n -v | head -20
 241: +```
 242: +
 243: +Both chains must show DROP rules at the bottom + the allowlisted IPs as ACCEPT. Without this step, the deploy claims egress controls but they are NOT enforced — `/transparency`'s `enforcement_layer` field would be inaccurate.
 244: +
 245: +## §2 — Smoke test (T-3 before demo)
 246: +
 247: +Authenticate, submit a clinical question, verify the bundle round-trips:
 248: +
 249: +```bash
 250: +TOKEN=$(curl -fsS -X POST https://polaris.<your-domain>/api/v6/auth/login \
 251: +    -H 'content-type: application/json' \
 252: +    -d '{"username":"carney_office","password":"<reviewer-pw>"}' \
 253: +    | jq -r .access_token)
 254: +
 255: +# Submit a run.
 256: +RUN_ID=$(curl -fsS -X POST https://polaris.<your-domain>/api/v6/runs \
 257: +    -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
 258: +    -H 'content-type: application/json' \
 259: +    -d '{"template":"clinical","question":"Is tirzepatide effective for type 2 diabetes?"}' \
 260: +    | jq -r .run_id)
 261: +echo "Run: $RUN_ID"
 262: +
 263: +# Tail the event stream.
 264: +curl -N -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
 265: +    https://polaris.<your-domain>/api/v6/stream/$RUN_ID
 266: +
 267: +# Fetch the signed bundle.
 268: +curl -fsS -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
 269: +    -o bundle.tar.gz \
 270: +    https://polaris.<your-domain>/api/v6/runs/$RUN_ID/bundle.tar.gz
 271: +tar -xzf bundle.tar.gz
 272: +
 273: +# Verify the GPG signature from the operator workstation (NOT from inside
 274: +# the deploy — the whole point is independent verification).
 275: +cd audit_*/
 276: +curl -fsS https://polaris.<your-domain>/transparency/pubkey.asc | gpg --import
 277: +gpg --verify manifest.yaml.asc manifest.yaml
 278: +```
 279: +
 280: +Expect: `Good signature from "POLARIS Carney Demo <signing@polaris.local>"`.
 281: +
 282: +## §3 — Live-submission rehearsal (T-2 before demo, I-carney-006)
 283: +

 297: +## §4 — Live demo (T=0)
 298: +
 299: +1. Open https://polaris.<your-domain>/ in browser, log in as `carney_office`.
 300: +2. Show the home / intake screen.
 301: +3. Submit Q1 (tirzepatide) — narrate the scope gate + retrieval + verification phases as SSE events stream.
 302: +4. When the run completes, click into the Inspector — show source-tier mix, verified-sentence Inspector pane.
 303: +5. Download the bundle — verify the signature on operator laptop (NOT in browser; demo independence).
 304: +6. Navigate to `/transparency` — read aloud the sovereignty filter, evaluator models, egress allowlist. Hand Carney's office the URL.
 305: +7. Submit Q2 (pharmacare) — observe sovereignty cascade in real time.
 306: +8. Take questions.
 307: +
 308: +## §5 — Fallback laptop
 309: +
 310: +If the AWS deploy is unreachable (DNS, ALB down, etc.) during the demo:
 311: +
 312: +**One-time setup (T-3 before demo):** populate the laptop's `.env` + `/etc/polaris/static_accounts.yaml` from AWS Secrets Manager so the laptop matches the production substrate.
 313: +
 314: +```bash
 315: +cd /local/polaris
 316: +
 317: +# 1. Pull secrets from AWS Secrets Manager to the laptop.
 318: +sm() { aws secretsmanager get-secret-value --secret-id "$1" --query SecretString --output text --region ca-central-1; }
 319: +
 320: +cat > .env <<EOF
 321: +OPENROUTER_API_KEY=$(sm /polaris/v6/openrouter_api_key 2>/dev/null || cat ~/.polaris/openrouter_api_key)
 322: +SERPER_API_KEY=$(sm /polaris/v6/serper_api_key 2>/dev/null || cat ~/.polaris/serper_api_key)
 323: +POLARIS_JWT_SECRET=$(sm polaris/v6/jwt_secret)
 324: +POLARIS_GPG_KEY_ID=$(sm /polaris/v6/polaris_gpg_key_id)
 325: +POLARIS_GPG_HOMEDIR=$HOME/.gnupg-polaris
 326: +POLARIS_STATIC_ACCOUNTS_PATH=/etc/polaris/static_accounts.yaml
 327: +POLARIS_ETC_DIR=/etc/polaris
 328: +POLARIS_API_PORT=8000
 329: +POLARIS_WEB_PORT=3000
 330: +PG_MAX_COST_PER_RUN=5.00
 331: +EOF
 332: +chmod 600 .env
 333: +
 334: +# 2. Write static_accounts.yaml to /etc/polaris (mirroring EC2 host path).
 335: +sudo mkdir -p /etc/polaris
 336: +sudo chmod 750 /etc/polaris
 337: +sudo bash -c "sm polaris/v6/static_accounts_yaml > /etc/polaris/static_accounts.yaml"
 338: +sudo chmod 640 /etc/polaris/static_accounts.yaml
 339: +
 340: +# 3. Import the GPG private key into the local keyring.
 341: +sm polaris/v6/gpg_private_key_armored | gpg --homedir ~/.gnupg-polaris --batch --import
 342: +
 343: +# 4. Bring up the stack.
 344: +docker compose -f docker-compose.v6.yml down
 345: +docker compose -f docker-compose.v6.yml up -d --build
 346: +
 347: +# 5. Smoke test.
 348: +curl -fsS http://localhost:8000/health
 349: +curl -fsS http://localhost:8000/transparency | jq
 350: +```
 351: +
 352: +Localhost demo at http://localhost:3000. Auth + signing + sovereignty filter all behave identically to the AWS deploy since the laptop loads the SAME secrets. /transparency will show `region: "unknown"` (no AWS_REGION env) — disclose this if a reviewer asks why.
 353: +
 354: +## §6 — 30-minute internal rehearsal (T-1 before demo)
 355: +
 356: +Schedule a 30-min Zoom with at least one non-engineer:
 357: +- They follow the demo script as the "Carney's office" surrogate.
 358: +- Time each step. Target: full demo < 20 min (10 min buffer for questions).
 359: +- Verify the bundle GPG verify on their workstation (not yours).
 360: +- Verify the /transparency page is readable.
 361: +- Pad any unclear narration.

 364: +
 365: +Per Codex iter-1 P1-1: the sign-off must NOT pipe the static brief alone (that lets Codex see placeholders, not real evidence). Instead, copy the brief to a working file, RUN each curl/GPG/iptables command, and PASTE the actual outputs into the working file BEFORE shipping to Codex.
 366: +
 367: +```bash
 368: +# Copy brief to evidence file.
 369: +cp .codex/I-carney-007/carney_demo_signoff_brief.md \
 370: +   /tmp/carney_demo_signoff_evidence.md
 371: +
 372: +# Run each §3 command and append its output. Example for §3a:

 374: +echo "## §3a evidence (live transparency response):" >> /tmp/carney_demo_signoff_evidence.md
 375: +echo '```' >> /tmp/carney_demo_signoff_evidence.md
 376: +curl -fsS https://polaris.<your-domain>/transparency \
 377: +    >> /tmp/carney_demo_signoff_evidence.md
 378: +echo '```' >> /tmp/carney_demo_signoff_evidence.md
 379: +
 380: +# Repeat for §3b /health, §3c bundle Q1 GPG verify, §3d bundle Q2 GPG verify,
 381: +# §4 egress chain output. Then submit to Codex:
 382: +env -u OPENAI_API_KEY codex exec --skip-git-repo-check - < /tmp/carney_demo_signoff_evidence.md
 383: +```
 384: +
 385: +If the evidence file still contains `<your-domain>` or other placeholders, ABORT — Codex would otherwise sign off on the example template instead of the real deploy. Expected verdict: `verdict: APPROVE ship_decision: SHIP convergence_call: accept_remaining`. Anything REQUEST_CHANGES or `ship_decision: HALT` → escalate to I-carney-006 (live rehearsal) for the failing question.
 386: +
